coredata.dc:title: Photoresponsive N-CDs@Zr luminescent nanozyme for dual-mode optical detection of oxytetracycline

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Kuang, Zean
Li, Xiangyuan
He, Li
Yang, Zhouping
Yang, Jun
Wang, Xianxiang
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes are widely employed in colorimetric analysis due to their exceptional catalytic activity; however, single-mode detection often has limitations, including insufficient sensitivity and inaccurate results. In this study, we developed a novel hybrid nanozyme, N-CDs@Zr, which exhibits fluorescence and photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking properties. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) not only enhance photogenerated charge separation and improve the catalytic performance of zirconium (Zr) nanoparticles but also endow the hybrid with fluorescent characteristics. The resulting N-CDs@Zr nanozyme emits blue light at 463 nm, and can catalyze the oxidation of colorless substrates into a blue product. As a demonstration, a novel colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode optical sensor based on N-CDs@Zr nanozyme was developed for sensitive detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) with a low detection of limit (0.145 µM), which is applicable for OTC assays in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Colorimetric
Fluorescence
Detection
Oxytetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoresponsive oxidase activity of N-CDs@Zr nanozyme
Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs@Zr hybrid
Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of OTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2021ZDZX0011) and Sichuan Province Natural Science Foundation for Youths (2022NSFgr6690, 2022NSFgr6202).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM images of N-CDs@Zr. (b) Elemental mapping of C, N, O, and Zr in N-CDs@Zr. (c) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs@Zr. (d) XPS survey spectra of N-CDs@Zr, and (e) corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of the Zr 3d region. (f) Absorbance, excitation, and emission wavelengths of N-CDs@Zr.

UV–vis spectra of N-CDs@Zr with TMB (dark line), N-CDs@Zr (red line) and TMB (blue line) in dark (a) and under UV light (b). The inserts show the corresponding photos. The UV irradiation time was 10 min. (c) The Michaelis-Menten curve fit and (d) Lineweaver–Burk plot for various concentrations of TMB. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Relative activity of different scavengers on the N-CDs@Zr/TMB system (n = 3). (b) EPR spectra of N-CDs@Zr under UV light (365 nm), pH = 4. (c) EPR spectra of N-CDs@Zr. (d) Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism based on N-CDs@Zr.

(a) UV–vis spectra of OTC of N-CDs@Zr + TMB, N-CDs@Zr + OTC + TMB, and OTC + TMB. (b) Absorption spectra of the N-CDs@Zr system at different OTC concentrations. (c) Linear fit of absorbance versus OTC concentration. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of OTC, N-CDs@Zr, and the combined N-CDs@Zr and OTC system. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of the N-CDs@Zr aqueous solution with and without OTC. (f) Relationship between F/F0 ratios and concentrations of OTC.

Selectivity and anti-interference studies of our optical sensor for OTC detection. (a) Relative response of the sensor to OTC compared with various potential interfering substances. (b) Anti-interference performance of the N-CDs@Zr sensor for OTC detection in the presence of metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) and biomolecules (methionine, glycine, glutamate, tryptophan, arginine, streptomycin, lincomycin, azithromycin, tobramycin, and penicillin) (n = 3). The concentration of OTC and other substances was 5 mM.

Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the N-CDs@Zr hybrid nanozyme (a), its photoreactive oxidase-like activity (b), and its application in colorimetric and fluorescence determination of OTC (c).
Determination of OTC in four samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Photoresponsive N-CDs@Zr luminescent nanozyme for dual-mode optical detection of oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130 Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A reliable colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection method for oxytetracycline was developed based on a photoresponsive N-CDs@Zr luminescent nanozyme.

Nanozymes are widely employed in colorimetric analysis due to their exceptional catalytic activity; however, single-mode detection often has limitations, including insufficient sensitivity and inaccurate results. In this study, we developed a novel hybrid nanozyme, N-CDs@Zr, which exhibits fluorescence and photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking properties. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) not only enhance photogenerated charge separation and improve the catalytic performance of zirconium (Zr) nanoparticles but also endow the hybrid with fluorescent characteristics. The resulting N-CDs@Zr nanozyme emits blue light at 463 nm, and can catalyze the oxidation of colorless substrates into a blue product. As a demonstration, a novel colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode optical sensor based on N-CDs@Zr nanozyme was developed for sensitive detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) with a low detection of limit (0.145 µM), which is applicable for OTC assays in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a widely used antibiotic for treating various bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, and skin infections. However, its misuse in the livestock industry and the subsequent potential environmental pollution have raised significant concerns [1]. Therefore, there is a high demand for rapid and accurate quantitative sensing strategies. Current methods for the detection of OTC include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [2], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4]. These methods possess certain sensitivity and accuracy but typically require complex sample pretreatment and specialized operation, making them unsuitable for rapid on-site detection. Although fluorescence [5,6], colorimetric [7] and electrochemical [8] methods have been developed, they primarily focus on a single-mode detection, which may lead to inaccurate results and false positives. In this context, dual-mode or multi-mode sensing methods are being developed to receive more reliable results with improved detection accuracy.

Nanozymes, a class of non-biological catalysts that mimic the active site structures and catalytic functions of natural enzymes, are opening new avenues for application in antibiotic determination [9–13]. Among the families of tremendous nanozymes, those photoresponsive ones, such as Zr-based nanomaterials, have gained significant attention due to their photocatalytic activity in addition to mimicking natural enzymes [14–16]. For example, Li et al. developed a MOF-525 nanozyme, a catalyst that activates peroxysulfate to facilitate the complete degradation of acetaminophen [15]. In another study, Li et al. created a Cu@Zr nanozyme sensor, which exhibits high sensitivity to urease activity through a process of self-assembly [16]. To improve catalytic efficiency, researchers have employed structural optimization and surface modification to promote the effective separation of electron-hole pairs and reduce their recombination, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity [17]. In this regard, carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated their capacity to improve charge extraction rate and reduce recombination [18]. In addition, CDs are metal-free nanomaterials renowned for their excellent fluorescence properties, making them highly versatile for a wide range of applications, including bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [19,20]. Therefore, constructing a complex that integrates CDs and photoresponsive nanozymes for the determination of antibiotics is highly promising, as it not only enhances catalytical efficiency but also imparts the complex with superior fluoresce properties from CDs [21,22].

In this work, we combined the outstanding luminescence of N-doped CDs (CDs) and the photoresponsive Zr-based nanoparticles were combined to create a N-CDs@Zr hybrid for the colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensing of OTC. The N-CDs@Zr hybrid was obtained via a two-step hydrothermal method, with the oxidase-like activity and obvious blue emission. On the one hand, the N-CDs@Zr hybrid catalyzed the dissolved O2 to generate superoxide anions under UV light irradiation, which then oxidized colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) with strong absorption at 652 nm. It was observed that OTC could diminish the blue color and reduce the absorbance at 652 nm. Additionally, the blue emission of N-CDs@Zr hybrid could be quenched by OTC mainly due to the inner filter effect. This work presents an efficient strategy for functional improvement of nanozymes, providing a facile method for the determination of OTC in real samples.

Zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4, 98 %), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2, 98 %), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC, 95 %) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99 %) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical. Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), acetic acid (HAc, 99.8 %), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc), ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), sodium azide (NaN3), thiourea (TH), EDTA disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na, 99 %), and p-benzoquinone (PBQ, 99 %) were obtained from Kelong Chemical Co, Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Fresh pork was purchased from a local supermarket. All chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification. All solutions used in this study were prepared with Milli-Q water.

The crystal, morphological structure, and elemental composition were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, DX-2700, Dandong, China), scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB-250Xi, China), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker vertex 70, China). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (A390, China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instrument Co. Ltd., Llantrisant, UK). Zeta potential data were collected with a Malvern Zetasizer apparatus (Zetasizer WT, UK). Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were conducted on the Bruker A300 spectrometer.

The N-CDs@Zr nanozyme was prepared via a two-step hydrothermal method. First, the N-doped CDs stock solution was prepared as follows: 1 g of citric acid (4.75 mmol) was added to 12.5 mL of H2O and stirred for 10 min. Then, 2 mL EDA (∼30 mmol) was added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 20 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C to remove impurities. The resulting yellow supernatant was collected and added to another reaction kettle containing 0.233 g ZrCl4 (1 mmol) and 0.181 g BDC-NH2 (1 mmol) in 25 mL DMF. After reacting at 120 °C for 24 h, the N-CDs@Zr nanozyme was obtained. Finally, the product was washed three times with DMF and ethanol, followed by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C, and vacuum-dried at 65 °C overnight for further use [23,24].

Zr-based MOF UIO66-NH2 was synthesized according to a previous report [25]. In brief, a mixture of ZrCl4 (0.233 g, 1 mmol) and BDC-NH2 (0.181 g, 1 mmol) was added to 25 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stirred for 10 min to ensure sufficient dissolution. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL reaction kettle autoclave and reacted at 120 °C for 24 h. The product was washed three times with DMF and ethanol, followed by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. Finally, the resluting UIO66-NH2 was vacuum-dried at 65 °C overnight for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoresponsive oxidase activity of N-CDs@Zr nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para:
The oxidase-like activity of N-CDs@Zr was evaluated using the typical oxidase substrate TMB. These oxidase-like nanozymes can catalyze the oxidase of TMB to oxidized form oxTMB, resulting in a color change that can be measured by UV–vis spectroscopy. In this work, 100 μL of N-CDs@Zr (ranging from 0.1 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL) and 100 μL of 5 mM TMB (dissolved with DMSO) were added to 800 μL of 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (ABS) buffer (pH = 3.0 ∼ 7.0). The resulting mixture was incubated in a water bath (10 − 70 °C) together with an irradiation with a UV lamp (365 nm) for a certain time. Finally, the absorption spectrum of the resulting solution was measured and the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded. The oxidase-like activity kinetic tests were conducted by adjusting the concentration of TMB [26]. Km and Vmax were calculated using the Michaelis-Menten equation:
(1)
v =
V
m a x
×
[ S ]
/
(
k m
+
V
max
)
where Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity, [S] is the substrate concentration, and Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant.

To perform colorimetric measurements, a mixture was prepared by mixing 100 μL of TMB, 100 μL N-CDs@Zr aqueous solution, 700 μL of ABS buffer and 100 μL of various concentrations of OTC. The mixture was incubated at room temperature under UV irradiation at 365 nm for 10 min. The absorbance of the resulting solution was then measured at 652 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

For fluorescence measurements, 100 μL of 1 mg/mL N-CDs@Zr aqueous solution was added to 800 μL of ABS buffer. Subsequently, 100 μL of OTC solution with varying concentrations (0 – 100 nM) was added. The mixture was vortexed thoroughly before fluorescence measurements were taken. The fluorescence spectrophotometer setting included an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and a slit width of 2.5 nm, with corresponding emission spectra recorded in the range of 400–700 nm. Selectivity experiments were conducted under the same conditions by replacing OTC with other antibiotics.

Pork and shrimp were purchased from local supermarkets, while water and soil samples were collected from our campus. An appropriate amount of pork and shrimp meat were cut into slices and beaten well with a homogenizer, soil samples sieved through 60 meshes. Then add the sodium dihydrogen phosphate based extract and mix well. Subsequently, 25 ml of the sample were meticulously mixed with 25 ml of Na2EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4.0) within a 50-mL centrifuge tube. Following this, the mixture was subjected to thorough agitation, and the resultant supernatant fluid was obtained by centrifugation of five minutes. Water samples were also centrifuged. Finally, the resultant supernate was filtered through a 0.22-µm filter membrane to yield the desired sample, which was then stored at 4 °C. It should be noted that the other samples were obtained in a similar manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs@Zr hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of N-CDs@Zr was carried out using a two-step hydrothermal method, as shown in Scheme 1 . First, N-CDs were synthesized to serve as the raw material. Then, the mixture of N-CDs and Zr-based precursors was incubated at 120 °C in a Teflon bottle. After purification and vacuum drying, a rose-pink powder was obtained, which dispersed easily in water. The morphology and microstructure of N-CDs@Zr were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1 a. The SEM images revealed a loose structure at the microscopical level and a network-like morphology with numerous Zr nanoparticles attached. The Zr nanoparticles had a size of approximately 40 nm, consistent with a small-size Zr-based UIO66 metal organic framework [27]. Elemental mapping images (Fig. 1b and S1) demonstrate that all the elements of C, N, O and Zr were uniformly distributed throughout the sample framework, supporting the successful combination of N-doped CDs and Zr nanoparticles. The XRD characterization (Fig. S2) shows different diffraction patterns of N-CDs@Zr and UIO66-NH2, supporting the successful fabrication of nanohybrid [28]. The surface ligand information of N-CDs@Zr was investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in the Fig. 1c, the peak at 3430 cm−1 corresponds to the overlapping N-H and O-H stretching vibrations [29]. The characteristic band at 1652 cm−1 is attributed to the C=C stretching vibration [30]. The intensity of the band at 1255 cm−1 is assigned to the C-N stretching vibration. The characteristic band at 1558 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate anions, while the bands at 1436 and 1377 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate anions [31]. The peak at 771 cm−1 is assigned to the O-H vibration of the terephthalic acid ligand, and the peaks at 661 and 507 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of Zr-O bonds [32].

To analyze the oxidation states and binding energies of N-CDs@Zr composite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1d) indicates the presence of C, N, O and Zr. The high resolution of Zr 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows two peaks: 185.08 eV corresponding to the Zr 3d3/2 and 182.68 eV ascribed to the Zr 3d5/2, indicating the presence of a typical valence state of Zr4+ [33]. In XPS C 1 s spectrum, three diffraction peaks (Fig. S3) at 284.5 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.3 eV correspond to C-C, C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively [34]. The enlarged O 1 s (Fig. S4) reveals three peaks at 530.0 eV, 531.5 eV and 532.4 eV, representing C-O, C=O and Zr-O, respectively, suggesting the presence of adsorbed oxygen, oxygen vacancy and crystalline oxygen [35]. The oxygen vacancies can act as electron traps, facilitating the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [36], reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby increasing the number of active intermediates in the photocatalytic process and enhancing catalytic efficiency.

The optical properties of N-CDs@Zr hybrid were explored using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 1f, the absorption peak of N-CDs@Zr is located at approximately 350 nm; the emission peak of 463 nm is excitation-independent (Fig. S5) and fluorescence lifetime is 1.93 ns (Fig. S6). Intuitively, the N-CDs@Zr shows a bright blue emission under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1f, inset).

To investigate the oxidase-mimicking activity of as-prepared N-CDs@Zr nanohybrid, the typical chromogenic substrate TMB was employed to mix with the PBS buffer containing N-CDs@Zr. It is well known that the TMB is colorless and can be converted to blue oxTMB in the presence of an oxidase. Thus, absorbance intensity at 652 nm of oxTMB was used to assess the oxidase activity of the nanozyme [37]. As shown in Fig. 2 a, without irradiation, the colors of TMB, N-CDs@Zr, and the mixture of TMB and N-CDs@Zr were all colorless, and their corresponding absorption spectra did no significant characteristic peaking at about 652 nm, which indicated that the N-CDs@Zr did not show the oxidase-mimicking property. With irradiation (with a UV lamp at 365 nm) for 10 min, the obvious blue color and peak at 652 nm strongly proven that the photo-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of N-CDs@Zr (Fig. 2b). The enzymatic activity of N-CDs@Zr was evaluated with TMB under UV irradiation rather than a xenon lamp, because the TMB solution containing N-CDs@Zr turns blue so fast (within 2 min, Fig. S7). Additionally, we suggest that the light-activated oxidase-mimic property originates from the Zr nanoparticles rather than N-doped CDs (Fig. S8 and Fig. S9) [38,25].

The oxidase-mimicking properties of N-CDs@Zr were influenced by reaction pH, temperature, concentrations and irradiation time. As shown in Fig. S10a, when the pH ranged from 3 to 7, the absorbance reached its peak when the pH was 4. Similarly, the temperature optimization results (Fig. S10b) suggested that N-CDs@Zr exhibits the best catalytic performance at 40 °C. The concentration optimization (Fig.S10c) revealed that the absorbance increased most significantly before the material concentration reached 0.1 mg/mL, remaining nearly constant thereafter. Fig. S10d showed that the reaction had reached equilibrium after 14 min. To achieve precise measurements, the absorbance was controlled within the range of 0.2 to 0.8, and the UV irradiation time was standardized to 10 min. Consequently, the optimal conditions for the oxidase-mimicking properties of N-CDs@Zr were established as follows: pH = 4, temperature = 40 °C, concentration = 0.1 mg/mL, and irradiation time = 10 min.

As shown in Fig. 2c, the steady-state well followed the typical Michaelis-Menten equation (equation (1) in the tested concentration range of TMB, with the R 2 of 0.986. From the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots (Fig. 2d), the corresponding kinetic parameters of initial velocity (Vmax , 0.59 × 10−4 mM/s) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km, 0.247 mM) were calculated from the slopes and intercepts of the fitted lines. Especially, the Km value obtained were better than some other nanozymes reported (Table S1). In addition, N-CDs@Zr showed good reproducibility and stability.

As shown in Fig. S11a, four batches of N-CDs@Zr hybrid were prepared and their oxidase activities were tested with an RSD value of 2.36 %. In addition, a batch of samples is sampled every 10 days to test their oxidase activity (Fig. S11b), an RSD value of 2.5 % was obtained. At the same time, the good salt stability of N-CDs@Zr was tested (Fig. S11c). These results above suggested that the N-CDs@Zr hybrid would be a good alternative for oxidase mimics.

Similar to most previously reported oxidase-like nanozymes, N-CDs@Zr hybrid as oxidase mimetics with oxygen as electron acceptor might generate reactive oxygen species (ROS, e.g. •OH, 1O2, •O2 −) to oxidize TMB [39]. To examine this hypothesis, various radical scavengers were used to confirm the presence of different ROS. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) was used as a scavenger for oxygen vacancies (Ovs), p-benzoquinone (PBQ) as a scavenger for superoxide anions, and thiourea (TH) and sodium azide (NaN3) as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen, respectively. Upon adding 100 μL (20 mM) of EDTA, p-benzoquinone, sodium azide, and isopropanol to the system (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S12), the absorbance in the EDTA and p-benzoquinone groups was significantly lower than that of the control group. This result suggests that N-CDs@Zr hybrid possesses oxygen vacancies and generates superoxide anions upon UV light absorption.

Moreover, ESR measurements further confirmed the types of radicals generated. Fig. 3b shows the ability of O2 .− to be directly captured by DMPO, forming the DMPO/ O2 .− adduct. At 0 min, O2 .− generation was not detected, but after irradiation, the appearance of a six-peak signal spectrum (four large peak, two small peak) confirmed the production of O2 .− [40].The intensity of the signal spectrum increased with longer irradiation times, indicating the generation of more O2 .−. The presence of single-electron Ovs was evidenced via ESR charaterization with a signal at g = 2.004, as shown in Fig. 3c [41]. The possible photocatalytic mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 3d. The formation of oxygen vacancies facilitates oxygen adsorption. The electrons separated from electron-hole pairs are captured by oxygen vacancies and react with oxygen to generate superoxide anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: OTC is a broad-spectrum antibiotic, and its large residues seriously affect public health. To protect environment and food production from the harmful effects of OTC, it is of great significance to develop a facile assay strategy. As a good oxidase-mimic nanozyme, N-CDs@Zr hybrid was employed for a colorimetric and fluorescence assay of OTC.

In the presence of OTC, the absorbance of N-CDs@Zr and TMB system significantly reduced, demonstrating the theoretical feasibility of colorimetric detection (Fig. 4 a). In addition, OTC inhibits the N-CDs@Zr catalytical oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB (Fig. 4a, inset). As the concentration of OTC increases from 0 to 0.8 mM, the absorption spectra were collected in the range of 550 − 750 nm (Fig. 4b). Clearly, as the concentration of OTC increased, the absorbance of TMB decreased, and the blue color gradually faded. The relationship between the absorbance and the concentration of OTC (Fig. 4c) follows linear equations Y = −2.427X + 0.99, (X = 0 ∼ 0.2 mM, R2 = 0.997) and Y = −0.605X + 0.541 (X = 0.2 ∼ 0.8 mM, R2 = 0.995), where X represents the concentration of OTC, and Y represents the detected absorbance at 652 nm. The limit of detection (LOD) for OTC base on
3 σ
/K was 5.04 μM.
( σ
i s
the variance of the intercept of the standard curve
.
k
i s
t h e
s l o p e
o f
t h e
s t a n d a r d
c u r v e )
. The two-stage linearity may arise from different reaction dynamics. At low concentrations (0.05 ∼ 0.2 mM), OTC rapidly reacts on the material surface, quickly saturating enzyme active sites and resulting in a steeper slope due to the material's high catalytic performance. At higher concentrations (0.2 ∼ 10 mM), product accumulation on the material surface slows the reaction, reducing the slope.

On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 4d, OTC could significantly quench the fluorescence of N-CDs@Zr hybrid. As the concentration of OTC increased from 0 to 10 µM, the fluorescence intensity obviously decreases (Fig. 4e). The linear equation between F/F0 and OTC was Y = −0.023X + 0.98 (R2 = 0.996) in the concentration range of 0.25 μM to 10 μM, where Y (F/F0) refers to the ratio of FL intensity between samples with and without OTC, and X refers to the concentrations of OTC. The LOD was determined to be 0.145 µM, which is better than some of existing reports (Table S2).

To investigate the detection mechanism, some experiments were carried out. As shown in Figs. S6 and S13, the absorption peaks of OTC coincide with the excitation peaks. However, the UV absorption peak of OTC and the emission peak of N-CDs@Zr do not overlap, indicating that the decline in fluorescence intensity is attributable to an inner filter effect rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer[42]. Zeta potential investigations (Fig. S14) indicated that the existence of electrostatic interactions between the positively charged N-CDs@Zr and the negatively charged OTC, which further enhanced detection sensitivity. The excitation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers in the photocatalysts were investigated through electrochemical testing. The photocurrent response (Fig. S15) of N-CDs@Zr was higher than that of UIO66-NH2, indicating that electron-hole pairs in N-CDs@Zr were more effectively separated than in UIO66-NH2. In addition to charge separation, charge transfer efficiency is also crucial. Electrochemical impedance characterization (Fig. S16) indicated more efficient charge carrier transfer of N-CDs@Zr. The above electrochemical experiments proved the photocatalytic effect of N-CDs@Zr and explained the reason for the generation of ROS, which is consistent with the EPR results. It's worth to mention that, due to the high electron density of typical functional groups such as double bonds, phenol groups, and amine groups in OTC, they are prone to attack by reactive species. Based on the above analysis and recent research, we predicted that OTC was degraded by N-CDs@Zr (Fig. S17) [43], which was also supported by the preliminary HPLC result (Fig. S18).

was used to determine OTC levels by spiking samples with different concentrations of OTC in fresh pork, water, soil and shrimp meat. The recovery rates of OTC were calculated ranged from 88 % to 109 %. These results demonstrate the practical potential of CDs@Zr hybrid for detecting OTC in real samples.

In conclusion, the developed N-CDs@Zr hybrid nanozyme demonstrates significant potential as a dual-mode sensor for OTC, combining colorimetric and fluorescence detection methods. The photoresponsive oxidase-mimicking and fluorescence properties enable effective catalytic oxidation of TMB, leading to observable color and fluorescence changes that facilitate sensitive detection of oxytetracycline. With detection limits of 0.010 mM for colorimetric and 0.145 µM for fluorescence modes, this sensing strategy offers high sensitivity and versatility. Finally, the method has been successfully used for colorimetric detection of OTC in pork and water, indicating that it has potential application prospects in the field of food safety.

Yang Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Zean Kuang: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources. Xiangyuan Li: Investigation. Li He: Supervision. Zhouping Yang: Data curation. Jun Yang: Methodology. Xianxiang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2021ZDZX0011) and Sichuan Province Natural Science Foundation for Youths (2022NSFgr6690, 2022NSFgr6202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The supporting information is available free of charge, including XPS, optical properties and optimum conditions of determination from Figs. S1-S16. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113500.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combined effects of azoxystrobin and oxytetracycline on rhizosphere microbiota of Arabidopsis thaliana

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhu, M. Ke, Z. Yu, C. Lei, M. Liu, Y. Yang, T. Lu, N.-Y. Zhou, W.J.G.M. Peijnenburg, T. Tang, H. Qian, Combined effects of azoxystrobin and oxytetracycline on rhizosphere microbiota of Arabidopsis thaliana, Environment International, 186 (2024) 108655.

Yu, Eu(III)-functionalized metal-organic framework as a visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for rapid discrimination and detection of multiple tetracyclines in agricultural products, Microchemical Journal, 205 (2024).
